FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, baseline ages 9-10, prospective follow-up to ages 11-13 at T2; recruited across 21 US cities

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective study with mediation analysis examining sleep duration, white matter, and depressive symptoms

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time variable is measured but not device-specific; abstract describes composite "screen time" without isolating phone or social media. Methods and measures section refers to screen time as a composite exposure without per-device breakdown isolating phone/SM activity. Other side pairs with sleep, white matter, and depressive symptoms (individual-trait-level outcomes), but the exposure itself does not meet the specificity requirement for phone/SM use.

notes: Paper is high-quality and reports mediation pathways, but the screen time variable lacks the required device-modality specificity (phone/SM isolation) per FT-IC2 conceptual criteria.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, waves 1-3 (ages 9-12 years), baseline sample 5,596 youths from 21 sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal mediation analysis examining sexual/gender minority identity, peer and cyber victimization, and suicidality

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Journal of Child Psychology and Psychiatry

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper examines peer victimization and cyber victimization as mediators of SGM identity → suicidality pathway. Cyberbullying (victimization) appears as a measured variable in a mediation model, but there is no separately-measured smartphone or social media use variable paired with an individual-trait-level outcome. Cyberbullying alone without a distinct SM/phone measure does not qualify per coder prompt (§25): "Cyberbullying alone (with no separately-measured SM/phone variable) does NOT qualify."

notes: Although the paper is well-designed and reports quantitative mediation results, it lacks a distinct smartphone or social media use measure. Cyber victimization is assessed but is not the same as SM/phone use modality.

FT_IC1.evidence: Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study, Release 4.0, year 2 data (ages 11-12 years), 3037 participants across 21 sites after QC filtering

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using principal component regression predictive modeling to examine multivariate relationship between 836 graph theory metrics and sleep duration, with examination of context and phenotypic associations

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed archival report published in Biological Psychiatry: Global Open Science, July 2025

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time measured and analyzed as a key socioenvironmental context variable predicting somatomotor disconnection (neural architecture). Results section (Fig 3A) and methods state: "greater somatomotor disconnection was associated with... longer screen times (β = 0.13, p = 2.6 × 10−19)." Screen time functions as an IV predicting a neural (individual-trait-level) outcome. Although methods do not isolate specific device modality, the construct measured aligns with screen-based media use in an adolescent sample.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric regression coefficient reported: screen time ↔ somatomotor disconnection β = 0.13, p = 2.6 × 10−19 (Figure 3A). Also reported: associations between somatomotor disconnection and cognition (β = −0.06, p = 4.8 × 10−5), externalizing symptoms (youth-reported: β = 0.05, p = 5.0 × 10−4; parent-reported: β = 0.05, p = .001), and school grades (β = −0.04, p = .007).

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple quantitative results reported for associations between neural and behavioral/cognitive phenotypes: externalizing (youth and parent report), school grades, general cognitive ability. All results include regression coefficients and p-values.

FT_EC7_reason: Design is cross-sectional (year 2 only), not longitudinal. FT-EC7 applies only to longitudinal SM-as-DV-only designs.
notes: Paper is INCLUDE. High-quality data-driven study linking screen time and socioeconomic context to brain network architecture (somatomotor disconnection) and cognitive/behavioral outcomes. All criteria met; FT-EC7 does not apply due to cross-sectional design.
